findings.description: Inverted TTL comparison (age < self.ttl) makes _is_expired report fresh entries as expired and stale ones as valid, so get() deletes fresh L1 entries on read and falls through to redis.get()+pickle.loads() every time, defeating the entire purpose of the L1 hot-path cache (correctness framing deferred to critic-logic).

self._local is an unbounded dict with no max size and only lazy per-access eviction; keys that are set() once and never re-read are never evicted, so high-cardinality key traffic is an unbounded memory growth / OOM vector (checklist 2.4: cache with no max size).
warm() loops calling set() which issues one pickle.dumps + one redis.setex per entry with no pipelining/batching, turning a bulk pre-population of N entries into N sequential Redis round-trips.

pickle.loads/pickle.dumps on every L2 hit and set is slower than json/msgpack for simple payloads and adds CPU cost on the cache hot path (security/RCE angle of untrusted-pickle deferred to critic-security).

get_or_compute() has no stampede/single-flight protection, so concurrent misses on the same hot key all execute compute() simultaneously (cache stampede / thundering herd) instead of one computing while others wait.
time.time() is invoked on every get/set/_is_expired call; negligible cost individually and listed only as a micro-optimization nit, not a real bottleneck.
